title: Benchmarking empirical and machine-learned interatomic potentials using phase diagram predictions for Lead
authors: Tom Hellyar, Pascal T. Salzbrenner, Peter I. C. Cooke, Chris J. Pickard, Scott Habershon, Livia B. Pártay
abstract: We compare the predicted phase behaviour of lead (Pb) using three different interatomic potential models, including an embedded atom method (EAM), a modified embedded atom method (MEAM), and a neural network-based machine-learned model in the form of an ephemeral data-derived potential (EDDP). Using nested sampling and replica-exchange nested sampling simulations, we computed thermodynamic and structural properties at pressures up to 60 GPa, mapping both melting behaviour and solid-phase stability. Both the EAM and MEAM models predict the face-centred cubic (FCC) phase to remain stable up to approximately 60 GPa. In contrast, the EDDP model captures the experimentally-observed FCC-to-hexagonal close-packed (HCP) transition at around 15 GPa. These results highlight the importance of training data and model flexibility in accurately describing high-pressure phase behaviour, and demonstrate the effectiveness of nested sampling as a robust framework for exploring phase stability in materials. Particularly, the combination of nested sampling with modern machine-learned interatomic potentials - delivering near ab initio accuracy at tractable cost - opens the door to truly predictive and exhaustive exploration. EDDPs trained on diverse, out-of-equilibrium configurations appear particularly well suited to this task, offering a robust and transferable framework for unbiased phase discovery.
summary: This work compares three interatomic potentials—EAM, MEAM, and a machine-learned EDDP—to map the high-pressure phase diagram of Lead. By using advanced sampling methods like Nested Sampling, the authors demonstrate that MLIPs are superior to empirical potentials because they accurately predict key solid-solid phase transitions. This validates the use of MLIPs combined with rigorous statistical mechanics sampling for predictive materials science.
main_problem: The paper benchmarks different interatomic potentials (empirical vs. machine-learned) to accurately predict the phase behavior and phase transitions of Lead under high pressure.
main_result: The machine-learned potential (EDDP) successfully captured the experimentally-observed FCC-to-HCP transition at $\sim 15 	ext{ GPa}$, whereas the empirical potentials failed to do so, highlighting the importance of model flexibility.
method: The study employs advanced simulation techniques like Nested Sampling (NS) and Replica-Exchange Nested Sampling (RENS) to calculate thermodynamic properties and map the Pressure-Temperature phase diagram.
model_or_system: The physical system is elemental Lead (Pb). The models compared are the Embedded Atom Method (EAM), Modified EAM (MEAM), and an Ephemeral Data-Derived Potential (EDDP), a neural network-based MLIP.
key_observables: Density ($ho$), Heat Capacity ($C_p$), Phase transition pressures (e.g., FCC $\leftrightarrow$ HCP), and relative free energy differences between solid phases.
important_parameters: Pressures up to 60 GPa, Temperatures, and the specific training data used for the MLIPs.
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on the assumption that the MLIPs can accurately represent the potential energy surface, and that the simulation methods (NS/RENS) provide an unbiased sampling of the phase space.
figures_summary: Figures summarize density comparisons, heat capacity vs. temperature curves showing solid-liquid and solid-solid transitions, and P-T phase diagrams comparing the predictions of the three potentials.
paper_structure: The paper systematically compares the predictions of EAM, MEAM, and EDDP across multiple thermodynamic regimes, using NS/RENS to calculate thermodynamic observables and structural order parameters to identify stable phases.
why_interesting: While focused on condensed matter, the rigorous application of advanced sampling techniques (NS/RENS) to calculate partition functions and phase stability is highly relevant to theoretical physics dealing with complex many-body systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (A) Density of solid lead at 300 K, as a function of pressure. Grey symbols show experimental results from Vohra and Ruoff [51], Mao [33], and Schulte and Holzapfel [52]. Coloured crosses are densities calculated from the NS calculations for the three different studied potentials. (B) Density of lead at constant pressure. Grey crosses show experimental results from Stankus and Khairulin [53], obtained at atmospheric pressure. Lines show the density from NS calculations at 100 MPa (dashed lines) and 10 GPa (solid lines) for the three tested potential models.

FIG. 2. Constant-pressure heat capacity of the simulated systems as a function of temperature, using the Pb-MEAM model. Solid and dashed lines represent calculations performed using standard NS and RENS, respectively. (A) Different pressures, with values displayed in GPa by each curve, with a 60-particle system size. Heat capacity values are shifted up by 0.01 eV K−1 for each increasing pressure, to assist readability. (B) Different system sizes (with atom counts shown by each curve) at 15 GPa. Heat capacity is shifted as a function of system size (increased by 0.005 eV K−1 for each successive system size). The 15 GPa, 60-atom NS curves in each panel (representing the same run) are shown...

FIG. 3. Schematic illustration of polytype face-centred cubic (FCC), hexagonal close-packed (HCP), and double hexagonal close-packed (dHCP) structures. ABC notation on the left marks the relative stack- ing position, with layers sandwiched by two different stackings in blue, and layers sandwiched by two sheets of atoms in the same rel- ative position coloured red.

FIG. 4. Configuration-averaged Steinhardt bond order parameters of an exemplar NS run (15 GPa, Pb-MEAM, 60 atoms). Values for raw NS configurations are shown by grey circles, while the order parameter values after the geometry optimisation are shown by black triangles. Optimal crystal structure values for FCC, HCP, dHCP and BCC are shown by horizontal dashed lines. The melting temperature at this pressure is marked with a vertical dotted line.
FIG. 5. Relative proportions of FCC, HCP, BCC and stacking vari- ants observed for different system sizes in 15 GPa MEAM NS runs and for a 60-atom RENS calculation.
